card_title: Bank says no fee. You still paid $300.

videos.video_title: How banks charge you nothing and still keep $300

hook_options: A business owner wires $10,000 to a Chinese supplier.
Why does 'no transfer fee' still cost you $300?
Your bank says zero fees. Your statement says you paid $300.
$10,000 leaves the account. $9,700 reaches the supplier. Bank says no fee.
Here is the trick banks use to charge you $300 without calling it a fee.
variants.body: A business owner wires $10,000 to their Chinese supplier. The bank receipt says zero transfer fee. Two days later the supplier confirms $9,700 landed. So where did the $300 go? It is the exchange rate. The bank quoted a rate about 3 percent worse than the real market rate. That margin is hidden inside the rate, so it never shows up as a fee. The supplier is short. Production pauses until you top up. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same $10,000 wire, you get the actual interbank rate, full amount lands with your supplier, no top-up email.
audience_feedback: Hook is weak. 'A business owner wires...' so what? Doesn't grab me. I scroll.
Wait, $300 missing on $10K? That actually happened to me last month. Real for me.
What is 'interbank rate'? You lost me. Just say 'real rate' or 'normal rate'.
I like the supplier-pause part. That is my biggest fear. Production stop because money short.
CTA 'Check out WorldFirst' too soft. Tell me what to do. Download? Website? Where?

hook_options: A business owner sends $10,000. The bank keeps $300 inside the exchange rate.
Two ways banks charge you: one you see, one you don't.
$10,000 wire. Visible fee: $0. Real cost: $300.
Banks quietly take 3 percent on every $10K wire. Here is the math.
$300 missing on a $10K wire. The receipt says zero fees. Both true.
variants.body: Here is the math. A business owner wires $10,000 to their supplier. Visible wire fee: zero. Sounds great. But the bank applied an exchange rate 3 percent worse than the real interbank rate. 3 percent of $10,000 is $300. That $300 is the bank's margin, baked into the rate, never itemised. The supplier receives $9,700, asks for the missing $300, production stalls. Run 12 wires a year and that is $3,600 a year you never see. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same $10K wire, FX margin around 0.4 percent, $40 instead of $300, supplier paid in full, same day.
audience_feedback: Hook is good. 'Both true' makes me curious. I want to know which is the trick.
$3,600 a year. Ouch. That is real money for me. I do about 10 wires a year.
'FX margin around 0.4 percent' I don't know what that means. Just say 'small fee' or '$40'.
'Never itemised' too big word. My English not strong. Use 'not on the receipt' instead.
'Search WorldFirst' is clear. I would Google it after this. Better than 'check out'.

hook_options: 'No transfer fee' is the most expensive fee your bank charges.
The cheapest-looking wire is usually the most expensive.
A business owner thought zero fees meant zero cost. It cost $300.
Banks love advertising zero fees. Here is why.
Zero fees is not the same as zero cost.
variants.body: Most business owners think zero transfer fee equals cheap wire. Flip it. Zero fee is exactly how banks distract you from the rate. Real example: $10,000 wired to a Chinese supplier, bank receipt says no fee, supplier confirms $9,700 received. The bank took $300 inside the exchange rate, 3 percent off the real market rate. They are allowed to advertise no fee because technically there isn't one. The cost is in the rate. That is why you should use WorldFirst. The rate you see is the rate you get, around 0.4 percent margin, no hidden 3 percent dressed up as no fee.
audience_feedback: Hook hits hard. 'Most expensive fee' makes me stop scrolling. I want to know.
'Flip it' I don't get. Flip what? Just say 'but actually' or 'the truth is'.
I never thought about this. My bank always tells me free transfer. Now I check.
'The rate you see is the rate you get' I like that line. Simple. I understand.
Story feels true but too perfect. Like a sales pitch. A bit suspicious.

card_title: Three business owners, same $50K wire, three supplier-end amounts

videos.video_title: Three business owners, same wire, three amounts

hook_options: Three business owners send $50,000 to the same supplier on the same day. Three different amounts arrive.
Same wire, same supplier, same day. Why did three business owners get three different results?
$50,000 out. $49,250 in. $49,700 in. $49,850 in. Same supplier, same morning.
Three business owners. Same factory. One paid $600 more than the others without knowing.
Here is why three business owners sending the same $50K wire all paid different amounts.
variants.body: Three business owners wire $50,000 to the same Chinese factory on the same Tuesday morning. Business owner A uses a major bank, supplier confirms $49,250 landed. Business owner B uses a regional bank, $49,700 landed. Business owner C uses a specialist provider, $49,850 landed. Same wire, same day, same supplier, $600 spread. All three receipts say 'no fee'. The difference is the exchange rate each provider quietly applied, 1.5 percent margin versus 0.6 percent versus 0.3 percent. That is why you should use WorldFirst. On a $50K wire, FX margin sits around 0.3 to 0.5 percent, supplier ends up with the most money.
audience_feedback: Hook is strong. Three people, same wire, different amounts. I want to know why.
Got a little lost with 'business owner A B C'. Maybe say their names or country. More real.
$600 difference is real money. That is like two days food for my family.
Three percentages thrown at me too fast. 1.5, 0.6, 0.3. Hard to follow on phone.
I trust this one. Feels like teacher explaining, not bank selling. I would search WorldFirst.

hook_options: Three $50K wires. Three rates. $600 spread, no fees on any receipt.
Bank A took 1.5 percent. Bank B took 0.6 percent. Specialist took 0.3 percent. Same wire.
$49,250 vs $49,700 vs $49,850. Same $50K wire, three providers, one Tuesday.
The difference between cheapest and most expensive on a $50K wire: $600. Same supplier.
Here is the math on three business owners and three identical $50K wires.
variants.body: Three business owners, same $50,000 wire, same factory, same morning. Run the math. Business owner A: bank applied 1.5 percent FX margin, that is $750 hidden in the rate, supplier got $49,250. Business owner B: regional bank at 0.6 percent margin, $300 hidden, supplier got $49,700. Business owner C: specialist provider at 0.3 percent, $150 hidden, supplier got $49,850. $600 spread between cheapest and most expensive. None of the three receipts mention any of this. Multiply by 12 wires a year, that is $7,200 lost on the wrong rail. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Specialist-tier rates, around 0.3 to 0.5 percent margin, every wire.
audience_feedback: Hook with three percentages too much for first 5 seconds. My brain freeze.
'Wrong rail' what is rail? I think train station. Confusing.
$7,200 a year lost is scary. I send maybe 15 wires a year. So $9K for me?
Too many numbers. Sounds like accountant talking. I want simpler.
I would screenshot this and show my husband. He handles wire. Useful info.

hook_options: The wire fee was never the expensive part.
Three business owners compared wire fees. They compared the wrong thing.
Same factory, same $50K, three different prices. The fee column on each receipt: zero.
Stop comparing wire fees. Compare what actually lands.
Three business owners thought they got the same deal. They paid $600 apart.
variants.body: Most business owners pick a provider by comparing wire fees. The fees match. They feel safe. Flip it. Three business owners send $50,000 to the same Chinese supplier on the same day. Wire fees: all zero. Amount that actually landed: $49,250, $49,700, $49,850. $600 spread, invisible on every receipt. The real cost was never the fee. It was the rate each bank applied behind the scenes. Comparing wire fees is comparing the wrong number. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Rate-first pricing, around 0.3 to 0.5 percent margin, what your supplier actually receives is the metric that counts.
audience_feedback: Hook is okay. 'Three different prices' makes me look. But too long sentence.
'Rate-first pricing' I don't get. Sounds like marketing word. Lost trust little bit.
I always look at the fee when I compare bank. This video changed my mind. Eye-opening.
'What actually lands' is good simple words. That is what I care about as importer.
Feels true. But also feels like setup for sale. A bit too clean story.

card_title: How a 2% bank-to-rail gap compounds over a year

videos.video_title: How a 2% gap compounds over a year of sourcing

hook_options: A business owner runs 12 wires a year to China. Watch what 2 percent does to their budget.
2 percent on one wire feels small. 12 wires a year is a different story.
$50,000 a wire. 12 wires a year. 2 percent gap. The math gets ugly fast.
A business owner thought 2 percent was nothing. By December, it cost $12,000.
Why 2 percent FX margin per wire wipes out $12K of sourcing budget a year.
variants.body: A business owner runs $50,000 wires to China, roughly one a month, 12 a year. Their bank applies a 2 percent FX margin on every wire. On one wire that is $1,000 buried in the exchange rate, invisible on the receipt. Easy to ignore. Now stack 12 wires. $12,000 of sourcing budget gone, no fee line on any of them. That money never reaches the supplier and never reaches the bottom line. It just sits inside someone else's spread. That is why you should use WorldFirst. FX margin sits around 0.3 to 0.5 percent, on the same 12 wires that drops the leak to under $3,000.
audience_feedback: Hook says 'watch what 2 percent does'. Okay I will keep watching. Works on me.
'Inside someone else's spread' I don't understand. Spread like butter? Confused.
$12,000 a year is one extra container shipment. That hits me. I feel it.
I do smaller wires, more like $20K each. Math doesn't quite match my life. Wish numbers closer to my size.
'FX margin sits around 0.3 to 0.5 percent' too technical. Just say 'much smaller fee'.

hook_options: $50K wire. 2 percent margin. $1,000 hidden. Times 12 wires. That is $12,000.
Here is the math on a 2 percent FX gap over a year of wires.
12 wires a year, 2 percent margin each: $12K. Zero fees on any receipt.
Run the year-end math: 2 percent times 12 wires = a missing $12,000.
$600K sourcing budget. 2 percent FX margin. $12,000 gone, no fee line.
variants.body: Here is the math. A business owner does $50,000 wires to China, 12 a year, $600,000 sourcing volume total. Their bank's FX margin: 2 percent per wire. 2 percent of $50K is $1,000. Times 12 wires: $12,000 a year. None of it shows up as a fee, all of it sits inside the exchange rate. Specialist FX rates sit at 0.3 to 0.5 percent. On the same $600K volume that is $1,800 to $3,000 a year. The gap, $9,000 to $10,000 every year, every year you stay on the wrong rail. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Specialist-tier rates, same wires, same suppliers, $10K back in your sourcing budget.
audience_feedback: Hook is just math. No story. I might scroll. Need more drama in first line.
'Specialist FX rates' too much business word. Just say 'we charge less'.
$10K back in budget is good. I would think about it for my next wire.
Too many numbers. $50K $600K 2 percent 12 wires 0.3 0.5 $1,800 $3,000 $9K $10K. Head hurt.
'On the wrong rail' twice now. Still don't know what rail means.

hook_options: You can't negotiate your way out of a 2 percent FX margin.
A business owner spent six months negotiating supplier prices. The bank quietly took it back.
The biggest line item in your sourcing budget isn't the supplier. It's the rail.
Stop negotiating supplier prices. Start negotiating wire rails.
2 percent feels small. So does a leak. Both sink the boat.
variants.body: Business owners spend weeks negotiating supplier prices for a 1 to 2 percent discount. Flip the lens. The bank takes a 2 percent FX margin on every wire and nobody negotiates with the bank. A $50,000 monthly wire on a 2 percent margin loses $1,000 every wire, $12,000 a year. That is bigger than the supplier discount they spent a month negotiating. The supplier sees none of it. The rail keeps it. Same wires, same suppliers, the silent leak is where the budget actually disappears. That is why you should use WorldFirst. FX margin around 0.4 percent, no months of negotiation needed, the saving lands on every single wire.
audience_feedback: Hook hits me. I spent two months last year haggling with supplier for 2 percent off. True story.
'The bank quietly took it back' wow. Never thought of it like that. Smart line.
Now I am angry at my bank. Maybe I will actually search WorldFirst.
'Rail' again. Three times in this batch. Please use 'transfer service' or just 'bank'.
Selling feels softer here. More like teaching me. I trust this more.

videos.video_title: $10,000 you lost this year and didn't know

hook_options: A business owner spent $500K sourcing this year. Their bank quietly kept $10,000 of it.
$500,000 went to China. $10,000 of it never left the bank.
Year-end review: $500K sourced, $10K missing, zero fees on any receipt.
A business owner closes the books at year end and finds $10,000 missing.
Here is the $10,000 your year-end accounts won't show you.
variants.body: A business owner spent $500,000 on Chinese sourcing this year, spread across 10 to 12 wires. Year end, they reconcile the books. The supplier got what was invoiced. The bank shows no fees on any wire. But running the rates against the real market, the bank's FX margin averaged 2 percent on every wire. 2 percent of $500K is $10,000. $10,000 that left the account but never reached the supplier. No fee line, no notification, just inside the rate. That is why you should use WorldFirst. FX margin around 0.4 percent, on the same $500K volume that drops the leak to $2,000, $8,000 stays in your sourcing budget.
audience_feedback: Hook is strong. 'Quietly kept $10,000' makes me feel cheated. I keep watching.
'Reconcile the books' too accountant word. I do my own books. Just say 'check the numbers'.
$10K is what I save for one year holiday. To know bank took it makes me sad and angry.
I do maybe $200K sourcing, not $500K. The math doesn't match my size but I get idea.
Story is good but ends like a commercial. Felt like real teaching for 20 seconds then sale.

hook_options: Math: $500K sourcing x 2 percent FX margin = $10K, every year.
$10,000 hidden in your rates this year. Here is how it adds up.
$500,000 in supplier payments. 2 percent margin per wire. $10,000 gone.
Year-end calculation: 2 percent of $500K supplier spend. That is the leak.
On $500K sourcing, your bank's 2 percent FX margin = $10,000 silent loss.
variants.body: Here is the year-end math for a business owner with $500,000 in Chinese sourcing. Bank FX margin per wire: 2 percent. Multiply: 2 percent of $500,000 is $10,000. That is the silent leak, baked into the exchange rate, no fee line on any wire confirmation. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5 percent margin. Same $500K volume: $1,500 to $2,500 a year. The gap, $7,500 to $8,500, every single year. Compound that on 3 years of growing sourcing and the leak is past $30,000. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same supplier list, same wire volume, specialist-tier rate margin, the leak shrinks immediately.
audience_feedback: Hook is just numbers and 'x' sign. Looks like math homework. I scroll.
$30K over 3 years is huge. That stopped me. Made me think.
'Compound' word too hard. I know compound interest but my friends don't. Use 'over 3 years'.
'Specialist FX rails' confused. Is WorldFirst a train? Lol.
'Same supplier list, same wire volume' I like this part. Sounds like easy switch. No big change for me.

hook_options: Your P&L will never show this $10,000 leak.
You won't find this $10,000 in your accounting software.
The biggest hole in your sourcing budget is invisible to your bookkeeper.
$10,000 leaked from your business this year and no audit will catch it.
If a vendor took $10K of your budget, you'd fire them. Your bank just did.
variants.body: Most business owners audit suppliers, audit logistics, audit staff costs. Nobody audits the wire rail. Flip it. A business owner with $500,000 in annual Chinese sourcing on a 2 percent bank FX margin lost $10,000 this year. The supplier got their invoice. Accounting matches. The leak is invisible to your bookkeeper, never on the P&L. If a vendor billed $10,000 a year for nothing, they would get fired the same week. The bank gets to keep doing it because nobody is looking at the rate. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Margin around 0.4 percent, same wires, the $10K stays in the business.
audience_feedback: Hook is the best so far. 'You'd fire them. Your bank just did.' I felt that. Punchy.
'P&L' is too technical. My P&L is just a notebook lah. Say 'profit report' maybe.
Yeah I would never let a supplier take $10K free. But my bank does it. I am angry now.
Story doesn't feel like a sale until the end. That works. I trust the message more.
I would search WorldFirst after this one. Hook stuck in my head.

card_title: Bank wire vs WorldFirst on the same $50K payment

videos.video_title: I sent $50K. My supplier got $48,500. Here is why.

hook_options: A business owner sends $50,000 to their Chinese factory. The supplier says only $48,500 arrived.
$50,000 sent. $48,500 received. Why?
Your bank says the wire cleared. Your supplier says they got $1,500 short.
Here is what happens to $1,500 of a $50K wire to a Chinese factory.
A business owner is short $1,500 on a $50K wire. Nobody stole it.
variants.body: A business owner sends $50,000 to their Chinese factory. Their supplier says they only got $48,500. The bank is eating about 2.5 percent. The exchange rate alone cost $1,200 to $1,250. Plus transaction fees. That is why the supplier ended up short. Now they refuse to start production until you wire again. That is a week of delay. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same $50K wire, you get the actual bank-level exchange rate, and same-day delivery. So check out WorldFirst.
audience_feedback: Hook is real for me. I had a supplier say money short. So stressful.
'The bank is eating' good word. Picture in my head. I get it.
Production stop until I wire again. Yes. That happened to me. Lost one week.
'Bank-level exchange rate' confusing. I thought I already use a bank. Now bank vs bank rate? Lost.
Two CTAs in 3 sentences. 'Use WorldFirst' then 'check out WorldFirst'. Too pushy.

hook_options: $50,000 wire, $1,500 missing. Here is the exact breakdown.
Sent $50K. Supplier got $48,500. The $1,500 gap, line by line.
FX margin $1,250. Intermediary bank fees $250. Total $1,500. On a $50K wire.
Bank quoted the rate 2.5 percent off market. That is $1,250 on a $50K wire.
$50K out, $48,500 in, 4 days lost. The receipt says no fee.
variants.body: Here is the math. A business owner wires $50,000 to a Chinese factory. The bank quoted the exchange rate about 2.5 percent worse than the real interbank rate, that is $1,250 hidden in the rate. The wire passes through two SWIFT intermediary banks, each deducts around $30, another $60 gone. Total leak: about $1,310. Supplier confirms $48,500 landed. They will not start production until they get the full amount, so the business owner has to wire again, with another set of fees. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same $50K, FX margin around 0.4 percent, supplier gets the full amount, same day, production starts.
audience_feedback: 'Line by line' makes me want to watch. Like a recipe. Good hook.
SWIFT? Intermediary banks? I just know I press send. Don't know all this middle stuff.
I do know about intermediary fees. My bank charged me $40 last time. Real for me.
Numbers don't quite add. $1,250 plus $60 is $1,310 but hook said $1,500. Confusing.
Hook said 4 days lost. Body doesn't say. Pay it off or drop it.

hook_options: The bank wire didn't just cost more. It cost a week of production.
Your supplier doesn't care about your bank. They care about $1,500.
A business owner thinks the bank is the safe option. The bank cost them four days.
The cheapest wire is the one your supplier gets in full, first try.
Banks compete on trust. Wires compete on what actually lands.
variants.body: Most business owners think a bank wire is the safe move. Flip it. Real example: $50,000 wire to a Chinese factory, supplier confirms $48,500 landed. The bank ate about 2.5 percent on the exchange rate plus intermediary fees, $1,500 short. Factory refuses to start production. The business owner wires again, more fees, four days lost. Bank wires don't just cost more money. They cost time and the trust of the supplier on the other end. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same $50K, FX margin around 0.4 percent, same-day delivery, supplier paid in full first time, production runs on schedule.
audience_feedback: 'A week of production' that hurts. I think customers waiting, money waiting. Stress.
'Trust' word at end is nice. My supplier did get mad at me before. Trust break is real cost.
I always thought bank is safe. Hearing this video, I am not so sure anymore.
'Flip it' word again. Don't get it. Maybe say 'but here's the truth'.
Sale at end is short. Just facts. I like this style. Feels honest.

card_title: Your sourcing budget shrinks 4% a year (silent compound)

hook_options: A business owner's sourcing budget shrinks 4 percent a year. Their supplier never raised prices.
Your sourcing budget is bleeding 4 percent a year. The leak isn't where you think.
A business owner spends $500K a year sourcing. $20,000 of it goes to the bank, not the supplier.
Same suppliers. Same volume. 4 percent of your sourcing budget vanishes every year.
Why a business owner's $500K sourcing budget loses $20,000 to nobody every year.
variants.body: A business owner spends $500,000 a year sourcing from China. Same factories every year, same products, same volume. The supplier hasn't raised prices. But the budget keeps shrinking. Why? Their bank's FX margin runs around 4 percent on every wire. 4 percent of $500K is $20,000 a year, baked inside the exchange rate, no fee line. Over three years, $60,000 compounded. You cannot negotiate that down with the supplier. You cannot squeeze it out of logistics. It is the rail. That is why you should use WorldFirst. FX margin around 0.3 to 0.5 percent, same suppliers, same volume, $17,000 a year back in the budget.
audience_feedback: Hook 'goes to the bank, not the supplier' good. Clear who is the bad guy.
$60K over 3 years is enough to buy a car. That number stopped me.
'Compounded' is too math word for me. Just say 'over three years' or 'adds up'.
'It is the rail' again. Three videos using rail. Please change word.
I trust this video. Doesn't feel pushy. Like a friend telling me.

hook_options: $500K sourcing x 4 percent FX margin = $20K. Every year. Compounded.
Year 1: $20K leaked. Year 3: $60K total. Same suppliers.
The math on a 4 percent bank FX margin over 3 years of sourcing.
$500,000 sourcing budget, 4 percent silent margin, that is $20K a year.
4 percent times $500K times 3 years = $60K. Inside the rates.
variants.body: Here is the math. A business owner sources $500,000 a year from China. Their bank's FX margin runs around 4 percent per wire, common for SEA banks. 4 percent of $500K is $20,000 a year. Year 2 the sourcing budget grows 10 percent, that is $22,000 leaked. Year 3 it is $24,000. Three years, roughly $66,000 inside the exchange rate, never on a receipt. Specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5 percent margin. On the same $500K that is $1,500 to $2,500 a year. The difference, around $17,500 a year, every year. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Specialist-tier rate margin, same wires, the leak shrinks the day you switch.
audience_feedback: Hook with 'x' and '=' signs. Looks like exam paper. I scroll.
'Common for SEA banks' okay this is for me. SEA is Southeast Asia right? I am in Malaysia.
Year 2 grows 10 percent then $22,000. Wait, how did 10 percent become $22K? Lost me.
'Day you switch' is good. Makes change feel easy. I want easy.
Too many percents. 4, 0.3, 0.5, 10. My brain is full.

hook_options: You can't supplier-negotiate your way out of FX margin.
A business owner cut supplier costs by 3 percent. The bank already took 4 percent.
Stop renegotiating with suppliers. Start renegotiating with rails.
The most expensive part of your sourcing isn't the factory. It's the wire.
Your supplier didn't raise prices. Your rail did.
variants.body: A business owner spends months pushing suppliers for a 2 percent discount. Flip the lens. The bank's FX margin sits at 4 percent on every wire and nobody negotiates with the bank. Same business, same $500K sourcing, that is $20,000 a year inside the rate, twice the size of the supplier discount they just won. The leak is in the rail, not in the factory. No amount of supplier negotiation closes a rail-margin gap. That is why you should use WorldFirst. FX margin around 0.4 percent, no monthly renegotiation, no supplier relationship damage, just a different rail underneath the same wires.
audience_feedback: Hook hits. I push my suppliers a lot. To know bank takes more than I save. Wow.
'Supplier relationship damage' is true. Last year my supplier got upset I kept asking discount.
'Renegotiate with rails' I still don't get rails. Bridge? Train? Tell me what is rail.
Punchy hook but body is more bla bla. Lost interest by middle.
First time I think 'maybe I should look this up'. The supplier negotiation point is too real.

card_title: How to read FX margin off your wire confirmation

videos.video_title: Find the hidden FX margin in 30 seconds

hook_options: A business owner pulls up their last wire confirmation. In 30 seconds, they find $1,175 missing.
Open your last wire receipt. We'll find the hidden fee right now.
Three numbers tell you exactly what your bank charged on your last wire.
Your wire confirmation hides the real cost. Here is how to find it in 30 seconds.
A business owner runs this check on every wire. Most never have.
variants.body: A business owner opens their last wire confirmation. $50,000 sent to a Chinese supplier. They find three numbers. One: the applied exchange rate on the receipt, 7.05. Two: the real interbank rate at the same minute, Google says 7.22. Three: the wire amount, $50,000. Math: subtract 7.05 from 7.22, divide by 7.22, that's 2.35 percent margin. 2.35 percent of $50,000 is $1,175. That is what the bank quietly took, inside the rate, never on a fee line. 30 seconds of math, $1,175 found. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Rate margin around 0.4 percent, same check, same $50K, leak drops to under $200.
audience_feedback: Hook 'open your last wire receipt' is fun. Like a magic trick. I want to play along.
Wait wait wait. Subtract 7.05 from 7.22 divide by 7.22. Too fast. I can't do this in my head.
I like that I can check myself. Makes me trust the video more. Not just selling.
'Interbank' word three videos in a row. Just say 'the real rate online' or 'Google rate'.
I might actually try this on my next wire. First useful tip I learned.

hook_options: Three numbers, one formula, your bank's FX margin in 30 seconds.
Applied rate. Interbank rate. Wire size. That is the whole audit.
Here is the exact formula to expose your bank's FX margin on any wire.
(Interbank minus applied) divided by interbank times 100. That is the hidden fee.
Open last month's wire. Run this 30-second check. See how much you paid.
variants.body: Here is the 30-second audit a business owner can run on any wire. Step one: pull the wire confirmation, find the 'applied rate', usually near the bottom. Example, applied 7.05. Step two: Google the interbank rate for the wire's exact date and time, example 7.22. Step three: subtract, divide by interbank, multiply by 100. (7.22 minus 7.05) divided by 7.22 times 100 equals 2.35 percent. That is the bank's FX margin. On a $50,000 wire that is $1,175 hidden in the rate. Run this on every wire from last year and add it up. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Specialist rates sit at 0.3 to 0.5 percent margin, the audit gets boring fast.
audience_feedback: Hook 'see how much you paid' makes me curious. Personal challenge. Good.
'Audit' is too business word. Just say 'check' or 'test'.
Step 1, 2, 3 is clear. I like clear. But three steps with math is too much for phone video.
'The audit gets boring fast' is a cute line. Made me smile. They sound human.
I would screenshot this and try it myself this week. Real action video.

hook_options: Your wire confirmation hides the real cost on purpose.
Banks don't print the interbank rate on your receipt. There is a reason.
If you don't audit the rate, the bank will keep charging it.
The fee column on your wire receipt is a distraction.
A business owner stopped trusting the receipt. Started trusting the math.
variants.body: Most business owners look at the wire confirmation, see no fee, close the tab. Flip it. The confirmation shows the applied exchange rate but never the interbank rate at the same minute. That gap is the bank's FX margin, designed not to be obvious. Open Google, type the currency pair, note the rate, compare. Example: applied 7.05, interbank 7.22, gap 2.35 percent. On a $50K wire that is $1,175 the bank kept, the receipt called it nothing. The audit takes 30 seconds. The fact that the bank doesn't run it for you is the whole point. That is why you should use WorldFirst. The rate you see is the rate you get, the 30-second audit becomes redundant.
audience_feedback: Hook 'there is a reason' makes me lean in. Like detective story. I keep watching.
'Currency pair' I think I know what that means. Like USD CNY. But not 100 percent.
'Designed not to be obvious' makes me angry at bank. Good for the video.
'Audit becomes redundant' two big words at the end. I had to read twice.
Best one for me in this card. Teaching feel. Not too much sale.

card_title: Good rate quoted vs real interbank rate

videos.video_title: What 'we'll give you a good rate' actually means

hook_options: A business owner asks their bank for a 'good rate' on a $50K wire. The bank says yes. The bank is right.
When your bank says 'good rate', they mean good for them.
'I'll get you a good rate.' Here is what that sentence actually costs.
A business owner accepts a 'good rate' of 7.05. Real rate that morning: 7.22.
Your bank just promised you a 'good rate'. Let's check the math.
variants.body: A business owner calls their bank to send $50,000 to a Chinese supplier. The relationship manager says, 'we'll give you a good rate today.' Bank quotes 7.05 USD-CNY. Sounds reasonable. Same minute, Reuters interbank shows 7.22. Gap of 2.4 percent. On $50K that is $1,200 hidden inside the 'good rate'. The bank didn't lie. It was a good rate, just good for them. The supplier still receives less, production still has to wait if the gap is meaningful. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Rate margin sits at 0.3 to 0.5 percent off interbank, the rate is good for the business owner, not the rail.
audience_feedback: Hook 'good for them' very clever. Made me laugh. I want to watch.
My banker tells me 'special rate for you' every time. Maybe now I check. Real for me.
'Reuters' I have heard the word. News company? But not sure how it tells rate.
'If the gap is meaningful' is weird sentence. Say 'if the difference is big enough'.
I trust this. Sounds true about banker conversation. Could be my Maybank manager talking.

hook_options: Bank's 'good rate': 7.05. Real interbank rate: 7.22. Gap: 2.4 percent.
$50K wire at 7.05 vs 7.22. The 'good rate' costs $1,200.
Here is the math on the bank's 'good rate' promise.
Run the numbers: 7.05 vs 7.22 on $50K. That is $1,200 in 'good'.
'Good rate' = 2.4 percent off interbank. On $100K that's $2,400.
variants.body: Here is the math on the bank's 'good rate'. Real interbank USD-CNY at 10am on a Tuesday: 7.22. Bank's 'good rate' offered to a business owner the same minute: 7.05. The gap, 7.22 minus 7.05, divided by 7.22, times 100, equals 2.4 percent. On a $10,000 wire: $240 inside the rate. On $50,000: $1,200. On $100,000: $2,400. Same percentage, scales with wire size. The 'good rate' is a relative term, relative to other banks, not relative to the real market rate. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Rate margin around 0.3 to 0.5 percent off real interbank, on $50K that is $150 to $250, not $1,200.
audience_feedback: Hook with two rates is easy to read. 7.05 vs 7.22. I see the gap right away.
'7.22 minus 7.05 divided by 7.22 times 100' too fast. Lost me at minus.
Three wire sizes example is helpful. I do $20K wires so I can guess my fee. Useful.
'Relative term' too fancy. Just say 'good compared to what?'
Last line 'not $1,200' is strong. Big difference, easy to remember. Good ending.

hook_options: 'Good rate' is the most expensive sentence in banking.
Banks compete on the word 'good'. You should compete on the number.
If a rate is 'good', ask 'compared to what?'
The 'good rate' is a marketing line. The interbank rate is a fact.
Every bank says 'good rate'. None of them mean the same number.
variants.body: Most business owners hear 'good rate' and feel reassured. Flip it. 'Good' is a comparison word with no anchor. Compared to what? Compared to a worse rate, yes. Compared to the real interbank rate, no. Example: bank quotes 7.05 USD-CNY, calls it a good rate. Same minute, Reuters interbank is 7.22. Gap 2.4 percent. On a $50,000 wire that is $1,200, inside the rate, never on a fee line. The bank competed on a word. The cost is a number. That is why you should use WorldFirst. The rate is quoted against real interbank, around 0.3 to 0.5 percent margin, comparison is against the actual market, not against worse providers.
audience_feedback: Hook 'compete on the number' is poetry. I like it but not sure my friends will get.
'Comparison word with no anchor' way too clever for me. What is anchor doing here?
'The bank competed on a word. The cost is a number.' That line is fire. I will remember.
Reuters interbank again. Three videos use this word. I still don't know what Reuters is.
Feels like a smart person talking down to me. A bit. But the punchlines are good.
